message.content: {"top_matches":[{"player":"otter-blue-1497","probability":0.72},{"player":"badger-red-827","probability":0.20},{"player":"peacock-pink-777","probability":0.08}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent, modestly generous proposals (e.g., offering small amounts of GREEN to buy RED) that create mutually beneficial exchanges — matches high agreeableness/communion and prosocial economic behavior.","Tendency to accept others' proposals even when the immediate payoff is small or slightly negative — consistent with high empathy, high ultimatum/acceptance rates, and social-desirability bias.","Conscientious and steady pattern of play (repeated similar offers across rounds) rather than large, opportunistic gambles — aligns with high conscientiousness and preference for fair, reliable interactions."],"uncertainty_notes":["Players’ private valuations drive behavior, so some actions that look prosocial may be purely payoff-maximizing given private values.","Limited number of rounds and some noisy choices (e.g., accepting a weak trade once) make definitive mapping uncertain.","High social-desirability score in the persona suggests stated preferences may emphasize fairness; observed behavior could reflect either genuine prosociality or impression management."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"owl-orange-1045","probability":0.6},{"player":"peacock-purple-2249","probability":0.25},{"player":"dog-purple-778","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness and communal orientation in my profile → I would favor cooperative, mutually beneficial trades and accept fair offers (owl accepted several beneficial proposals and realized steady gains).","Generosity and prosocial tendencies (high dictator/ultimatum generosity, high trust sender) → I would make or accept trades that help others as well as myself; peacock’s occasional generous offers fit this moderately.","Conscientiousness and mental-accounting tendencies → I would be selective about proposals, seeking clear gains and avoiding plainly bad deals; dog’s more aggressive self-interested bargain-seeking fits this least well."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only observed discrete choices and outcomes — motives (strategic vs. prosocial) are inferred and may be ambiguous.","Random selection when multiple players accepted confounds who actually intended to trade with whom.","Private valuations drive a lot of behavior; matching personality to limited trade actions is probabilistic, not definitive."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"swan-pink-1424","probability":0.6},{"player":"whale-green-1798","probability":0.3},{"player":"turtle-yellow-7601","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness/communion and generosity reflected in proposing and accepting trades that produced mutual gains (swan’s accepted deals often benefited both parties).","Conscientious, thoughtful play: swan’s proposals (offering low-value red to obtain higher-value green for herself) show deliberate, utility-improving choices consistent with high need for cognition and conscientiousness.","High social desirability and empathy align with swan’s cooperative pattern (willingness to make trades even when gains are modest and to accept proposals that help others).","Whale is a secondary match because of strategic, goal-directed behavior and repeated proposals that maximize own payoff—this fits the persona’s high conscientiousness and need for cognition but is somewhat more self-serving than the persona’s strong communal orientation.","Turtle matches least: passive/declining behavior and low engagement are inconsistent with the persona’s combination of high communion, empathy, and willingness to propose/accept mutually beneficial trades."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only observed in a short, strategic bargaining setting; private valuations and unobserved motives mean behavior may map imperfectly to personality.","Some actions (e.g., who accepts when multiple people would benefit) can reflect opportunism or momentary strategy rather than stable traits like agreeableness or empathy.","High social-desirability score in the profile suggests stated preferences for fairness that may not perfectly predict in-game strategic choices, adding uncertainty to the match."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"monkey-pink-6874","probability":0.8},{"player":"goose-purple-9985","probability":0.12},{"player":"buffalo-orange-6610","probability":0.08}],"behavioral_basis":["Repeated acceptance of offers that reduce own payout (e.g., accepted buffalo’s GREEN-for-RED offers that hurt monkey) matches high agreeableness, high ultimatum-acceptance, and strong prosocial/forgiving tendencies in the profile.","Made at least one clearly disadvantageous proposal (offering higher-value RED for lower-value GREEN) consistent with generous/altruistic behavior and high social desirability.","Profile shows high trust and generous dictator/ultimatum behavior (trust-sender 80, dictator 40, ultimatum-sender 60) which maps to monkey’s pattern of giving outsized concessions and accepting less favorable deals.","Low neuroticism and high conscientiousness/communion suggest steady, relationship-preserving choices rather than purely payoff-maximizing moves — monkey’s pattern fits this more than buffalo’s consistently self-interested proposals."],"uncertainty_notes":["Small number of observed rounds — behavior could reflect a momentary mistake or experiment rather than stable trait.","Players’ private valuations drive acceptance thresholds; identical acceptance behavior might stem from bounded rationality or misunderstanding rather than prosocial preference.","Goose shows mixed behavior (proposer greediness but one acceptance of a bad offer), so some features of the profile could map to goose in alternative interpretations."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"ram-pink-8623","probability":0.75},{"player":"otter-pink-5988","probability":0.15},{"player":"squid-yellow-4597","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Ram repeatedly made or accepted trades that reduced her own payout (e.g., accepted proposals that cost her money and proposed generous trades), matching high agreeableness, high generosity, and strong communal orientation in the persona.","Otter was more selective: accepted some beneficial trades and declined others, suggesting moderate cooperativeness but more cautious/strategic behavior—partially consistent with the persona's conscientiousness and fairness orientation.","Squid repeatedly accepted and proposed trades that increased their own payout, appearing more opportunistic/self-interested, which contrasts with the persona's high agreeableness and pro-social tendencies."],"uncertainty_notes":["Actions can reflect private valuations and short-term opportunism rather than stable personality; limited rounds provide a small behavioral sample.","Random selection when multiple acceptances occurred makes it hard to observe intended partner-choice motives.","We observe only revealed trade choices (no verbal reasoning), so inferred motives (fairness vs. mistake vs. strategy) are uncertain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"camel-pink-767","probability":0.6},{"player":"alligator-orange-9612","probability":0.3},{"player":"butterfly-yellow-2139","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness and communion in my profile map to cooperative, repeated small mutual trades; camel repeatedly proposed modest 1:1 trades that were accepted and also accepted others’ offers — consistent with a prosocial, helpful bargaining style.","My generosity and high trust (ultimatum/trust game tendencies) align with a player who makes reasonable offers that others will accept rather than extreme, opportunistic demands — camel fits this better than butterfly.","Alligator shows some cooperative behaviour (accepting beneficial offers, proposing trades that succeeded) but is more opportunistic (offers primarily benefit proposer); that makes alligator a secondary match.","Butterfly often proposed large, demanding trades that were declined and only accepted when clearly profitable for her, which fits a more self-interested/strategic profile less like my communal, caretaker-oriented personality."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed actions are shaped strongly by private chip values; similar external behavior can arise from different motivations, so match is probabilistic.","My self-reports show high social desirability which may bias how closely my stated preferences map to actual in-game choices.","Only three players and a short sequence of moves limit resolution — middle-ranked candidate (alligator) could plausibly be my behavior under a more opportunistic-but-cooperative interpretation."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"goose-green-3433","probability":0.65},{"player":"buffalo-green-2230","probability":0.25},{"player":"bear-green-5745","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Frequent acceptance of mutually beneficial offers and repeated successful proposals: goose accepted others’ offers and proposed trades that increased total payoffs, matching the respondent’s high agreeableness, empathy, and cooperative orientation.","Willingness to make fair/cooperative trades: buffalo’s proposals that generated modest gains for both parties are consistent with a conscientious, relationship-focused player who avoids zero-sum conflict.","More selective and self-interested behavior: bear often declined proposals and pursued trades favoring their own high-value chips (BLUE), which aligns less with the respondent’s highly prosocial, caretaker tendencies."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed actions are constrained by private chip valuations; some ‘‘selfish’’ moves may reflect incentives rather than disposition, so mapping behavior to personality is noisy.","Small number of rounds and proposals limits confidence — a few idiosyncratic choices can disproportionately affect the match.","Demographic and survey traits (e.g., high social desirability, prosocial game measures) inform the judgment but may not fully predict in-game strategic behavior."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"iguana-yellow-3262","probability":0.65},{"player":"zebra-pink-2457","probability":0.25},{"player":"cat-green-4185","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness / prosocial tendencies: iguana accepted several clearly disadvantageous trades (e.g., R0T0 losing value) consistent with a person who prioritizes cooperation and social harmony over strict self-interest.","Conscientiousness & caregiving orientation: iguana often participated and made trades that stabilized payouts across rounds, matching a dependable, caretaker-like approach from the profile.","High acceptance rate: iguana (accepted ~4/6 opportunities) aligns with the persona’s very high ultimatum acceptance and cooperative bias; cat accepted far fewer offers (more selective), zebra’s behavior was more opportunistic as proposer.","Generosity vs opportunism: zebra made large self-benefiting proposals (and gained larger per-proposal payouts), so is a secondary match for someone moderately cooperative but also strategic; cat’s pattern of repeatedly proposing highly self-favoring trades and selectively accepting makes her least similar."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only observed choices and payoffs are available—no direct measures of motives, feelings, or private negotiation thoughts; identical actions can arise from different motivations (e.g., risk tolerance, error, reciprocity).","Random selection when multiple acceptors existed can obscure whether a player was targeting cooperative or selfish outcomes.","Players may have adapted strategy across rounds; short game length (3 rounds) limits ability to fully characterize stable personality-consistent behavior."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"owl-green-168","probability":0.7},{"player":"iguana-blue-1059","probability":0.2},{"player":"squid-purple-9957","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Frequent acceptance of others' offers (owl accepted multiple trades) matches the subject's very high acceptance in bargaining (ultimatum accepted = 100%) and high agreeableness/communion (prosocial, relationship-preserving behavior).","Owl's pattern of accepting even modest gains (small net gains observed) aligns with the subject's tendency toward fairness, generosity (dictator = 40%, high ultimatum offers) and high social-desirability—willing to take cooperative options.","Iguana as a close second: made fair/prosocial-looking proposals (trading away low-value red to gain green) consistent with a conscientious, thoughtful player, but iguana declined many incoming proposals which is less consistent with the subject's accepting/agreeable profile.","Squid least likely: often declined others' proposals as a responder and only sometimes produced mutually beneficial proposals; this more independent/strategic pattern fits the subject less well."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavioral inference is limited because players have private chip values—accept/decline choices can be driven by private incentives rather than stable social preferences.","Only nine proposer turns observed; small-sample noise could make acceptance rates misleading.","Some matches hinge on interpreting acceptance as prosocial vs. simply self-interested (accepting when it yields any positive payoff)."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"turtle-red-9966","probability":0.7},{"player":"dog-green-8079","probability":0.2},{"player":"parrot-pink-5400","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently accepted disadvantageous offers from another player (accepted dog-green-8079 proposals repeatedly despite personal payout loss), matching high agreeableness, empathy, and the tendency to accept offers seen in the persona (ultimatum receiver = 100% accept).","Made proposals that were often rejected by others, suggesting lower strategic/reflective responsiveness (low CRT and low syllogistic scores in persona) and consistent behavior (very low self-monitoring).","Parrot and dog behaved more strategically/opportunistically (parrot never accepted offers; dog repeatedly proposed trades that profited himself), which fits less well with the persona's generous/cooperative profile but dog shows some consistent strategic responding, placing it second."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed actions are few rounds and may reflect short-term strategy or mistakes rather than stable personality.","Private chip values drive accept/decline decisions; some acceptances could be due to misperception or calculation errors rather than prosocial motives.","High social desirability in the persona could mean stated tendencies (e.g., willingness to help) do not always map perfectly to in-game choices, increasing classification uncertainty."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"bear-green-5745","probability":0.6},{"player":"goose-green-3433","probability":0.3},{"player":"buffalo-green-2230","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness, empathy, and generosity in my profile → likelihood to accept fair or neutral offers and to behave cooperatively aligns with bear’s early acceptance and non-predatory proposals.","Conscientiousness and social-desirability suggest preferring fair trades over opportunistic gains; bear’s neutral/mildly cooperative proposals fit this better than buffalo’s repeated attempts to extract value.","Goose made several profitable, assertive proposals and pursued gains actively; that partly matches a pragmatic, goal-directed side (conscientiousness) so goose is a secondary match.","Buffalo consistently rejected others’ offers and repeatedly proposed to capture red chips for personal gain, which appears more self-interested than my stated prosocial, caretaker-oriented preferences. "],"uncertainty_notes":["We only observe in-game choices and not stated motives; private chip valuations affect decisions and some acceptances could be due to indifference rather than prosociality.","High social-desirability score raises possibility I’d behave more cooperatively in an observed setting than privately — this could make me appear more like goose or bear depending on context.","Limited number of accepted trades (few executed trades) reduces confidence in distinguishing some behavioral patterns."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"cat-orange-2462","probability":0.65},{"player":"otter-blue-7516","probability":0.25},{"player":"peacock-red-3668","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness and communion in my profile aligns with cat's frequent acceptance of fair/neutral trades and modest, cooperative proposals.","My high generosity (ultimatum/dictator behavior) and tendency to accept offers maps to cat accepting others' proposals that were neutral/beneficial to partners.","Conscientiousness and rule-following (mental accounting) fit cat's steady, low-risk moves rather than aggressive profit-seeking.","Otter shows some opportunistic, payoff-focused behavior consistent with moderate need for cognition/strategic thinking but less communal than me; peacock appears most self-interested and strategically aggressive, least like my communal profile."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only observed revealed moves and public payouts; private valuations and motives are partially inferential.","Small number of rounds and some proposals were declined, providing limited behavioral signal.","High social-desirability in my profile could make my actual in-game choices look more prosocial than they would be in other contexts, adding ambiguity."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"badger-red-5032","probability":0.7},{"player":"camel-yellow-5577","probability":0.2},{"player":"squid-yellow-9545","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Badger accepted multiple proposals (cooperative/responsive): aligns with high agreeableness, empathy, and tendency to cooperate/accept fair deals.","Camel pursued consistent, pragmatic proposals that successfully benefited them: matches high conscientiousness, methodical strategy and goal-directed behavior.","Squid mainly proposed aggressive value-seeking trades and rarely accepted others' offers: less consistent with the respondent's cooperative, family-oriented and socially desirable profile."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed actions reflect private chip valuations as much as personality; accepting/declining can be driven by incentive structure rather than social preferences.","Small number of rounds and some randomness in partner selection limit confidence in mapping behavior to personality.","High social-desirability in the profile suggests the person might sometimes behave more cooperatively than incentives predict, which can blur distinctions among players."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"kangaroo-blue-9562","probability":0.65},{"player":"squid-purple-3630","probability":0.25},{"player":"butterfly-blue-7661","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness, trust and generosity in your profile best matches a player who makes deals that look attractive to others and accepts mutually beneficial trades — kangaroo’s proposals often produced reasonably balanced or partner-friendly payoffs (e.g., kangaroo’s BLUE4→RED3 deal gave the partner a large gain) and kangaroo accepted profitable offers when they benefited, consistent with a cooperative, pragmatic player.","Your high conscientiousness and practical orientation fit a player who pursues steady, concrete gains (kangaroo repeatedly targets RED, their high-value chip) rather than making erratic or highly self-serving moves. Kangaroo’s consistent, value-driven proposals align with that.","Squid is the second-best match because squid also made some partner-friendly proposals (e.g., offering RED5 to buy BLUE4 yielded a large partner gain) and accepted profitable trades — consistent with trust and cooperativeness. However, squid also made at least one implausible/very unbalanced proposal (BLUE1 for RED22) that seems less consistent with the user’s cautious, conscientious style.","Butterfly is the least likely match: many of butterfly’s proposals were strongly self-serving (large personal gains and small or no partner gains) and they declined several offers — this pattern contrasts with the user’s high agreeableness, generosity, and trust."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only a small number of rounds and observations are available; behavior could be strategic responses to revealed public information or to private valuations rather than stable personality expression.","Players’ private chip valuations heavily constrain what looks like generosity or selfishness; some proposals that look generous for the partner were simply profitable given their private values, so mapping to personality has unavoidable ambiguity.","High reported social-desirability and overconfidence in your profile add noise: you might behave more generously in observable settings, but private incentives in this game could still drive departures from that tendency."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"badger-green-4722","probability":0.8},{"player":"peacock-purple-5231","probability":0.15},{"player":"unicorn-blue-7096","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Repeatedly proposing trades that cost oneself (offering high-value GREEN to buy low-value BLUE) indicates high cooperativeness and generosity, matching the subject's high agreeableness, high ultimatum/dictator generosity, and 100% acceptance tendency.","Frequent acceptance of others' proposals (even when personally costly) aligns with the subject's very high ultimatum acceptance and social-desirability bias — willingness to go along for fairness/relationship reasons.","Peacock's pattern of making profitable proposals and mixed acceptance decisions suggests opportunistic but socially-aware behavior — somewhat plausible given moderate conscientiousness but lower match than badger.","Unicorn's consistent self-serving proposals and selective acceptance map to a profit-maximizing, lower-agreeableness profile, which is inconsistent with the subject's high communion and empathy."] ,"uncertainty_notes":["Only observed in-game behavior with known private valuations; motivations (altruism vs. strategic signaling) are inferred and may be ambiguous.","The subject's high social desirability could make them act more prosocially in an experimental setting than in real life, potentially exaggerating the match with ‘badger’.","Three-round, small-sample interaction limits certainty; probabilities reflect behavioral fit rather than definitive identity."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"dog-red-9959","probability":0.6},{"player":"otter-yellow-9944","probability":0.3},{"player":"bear-blue-8468","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Dog frequently proposed and accepted mutually beneficial trades (e.g. successful blue-for-purple trade) consistent with high agreeableness, trust, and preference for fair, cooperative outcomes.","Otter made some cooperative proposals but also extracted larger self-gains in at least one trade, which partly matches a conscientious, strategic player who nevertheless sometimes pursues self-interest.","Bear’s behavior was more self-serving and unsuccessful as a proposer (many declined offers), which fits less well with the profile’s high empathy, communal orientation, and cooperative generosity."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only three rounds of play — small sample of behavior and limited ability to infer stable preferences.","Players’ private chip valuations differ, so identical actions can reflect different motives (strategy, error, inattention, or prosociality).","High social desirability in the profile could make the real person behave more cooperatively than their private reasoning would predict, adding ambiguity when matching to observed actions."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"ram-red-1301","probability":0.7},{"player":"goose-orange-349","probability":0.25},{"player":"butterfly-orange-8262","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Ram accepted multiple others' proposals (including small losses), consistent with high agreeableness, high communion, high ultimatum/acceptance tendencies, and caregiving orientation in the profile.","Ram’s pattern of accepting proposals even when slightly disadvantageous matches the profile’s high trust/acceptance (trust game sender 80%, ultimatum accepted 100%) and strong prosocial/moral self-concept.","Goose shows some cooperative behavior (accepted one loss, made several mildly self-beneficial proposals), making them a plausible second match but less consistently prosocial than Ram.","Butterfly mostly declined proposals and was selective, which conflicts with the profile’s high agreeableness, high acceptance, and caregiving orientation, so they are the least likely match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only chip-trading actions (proposals and binary accept/decline) are observed — motives (fairness vs strategic sacrifice) are ambiguous.","Proposer behavior is inherently self-interested in this setting; a highly prosocial person may still propose self-beneficial trades while being more willing to accept others’ offers, which complicates matching.","Limited sample (three players, few rounds) means small differences in acceptance/proposal patterns can look large; probabilities reflect this uncertainty."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"whale-pink-487","probability":0.6},{"player":"iguana-red-1430","probability":0.3},{"player":"duck-red-8937","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Made proposals that were often profitable but not extreme/exploitative (consistent with high conscientiousness and desire for acceptable, pragmatic deals).","Had proposals accepted multiple times and avoided repeatedly rejected offers (fits a cooperative, fairness-oriented player who plans workable trades).","Accepted reasonable offers and sometimes proposed modest mutually beneficial trades, matching high agreeableness, high trust/generosity, and a tendency to be cooperative while still maximizing gains."],"uncertainty_notes":["Small number of rounds and hidden private valuations make inferred motives noisy.","High social desirability in the persona could make observed cooperative behavior exaggerated or strategic rather than purely prosocial.","One player (duck-red-8937) accepted most offers but made extreme proposals; acceptance frequency alone could point to that player, but proposer behavior and overall strategy favored whale-pink-487 as a closer match."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"ram-purple-8899","probability":0.65},{"player":"owl-yellow-9160","probability":0.25},{"player":"lion-yellow-9203","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent proposer and frequent accepter: ram repeatedly proposed modest mutually beneficial trades and accepted others' offers that produced small, reliable gains — matches high conscientiousness, cooperativeness, and preference for fair, low-risk exchanges.","Prosocial / fairness orientation: my persona reports fairness/kindness and shows high ultimatum/trust generosity; ram’s offers often created positive-sum outcomes for both parties, aligning with a fair, helpful bargaining style.","Owl shows opportunistic acceptance of very profitable offers and made aggressive self-benefiting proposals — partly consistent with pragmatic cooperation but less consistent with my high agreeableness and desire to help others.","Lion’s behavior is more selective and sometimes declines offers that appear profitable to them, which is less consistent with my high acceptance of fair offers and high social-desirability/cooperative tendencies."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed choices are a narrow slice of potential behavior; the same moves can reflect strategic calculation rather than stable personality traits.","Players’ private valuations drive much of the observed behavior; similarities could arise from incentives rather than underlying prosocial/conscientious tendencies.","Small number of rounds and a few surprising declines (e.g., lion declining profitable trades) make inference noisy; alternative weightings could reasonably place more mass on owl or lion."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"pig-green-499","probability":0.7},{"player":"swan-purple-7217","probability":0.2},{"player":"zebra-red-3364","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness and empathy reflected in making mutually beneficial proposals and accepting fair deals — pig frequently proposed trades that exploited others' higher valuation of PURPLE while still creating win-win outcomes.","High conscientiousness and need-for-cognition suggesting a strategic, persistent proposer who plans trades across rounds — pig repeatedly proposed and engaged in trades that improved payoff without appearing purely greedy.","Swan shows some cooperative/accepting behavior (accepted an unfavorable-looking deal, made reciprocal proposals) so is a secondary match for the prosocial aspects.","Zebra’s pattern of pushing larger, often-declined requests and ending with the highest payoff looks more self-interested and opportunistic, making it the least likely match."],"uncertainty_notes":["We don’t have the respondent’s private chip valuations, so inference relies on mapping game actions to psychological traits rather than direct preference alignment.","Players’ acceptance/decline choices can reflect momentary calculation, risk attitudes, or random tie-breaking; behavior in three rounds may not fully represent stable personality.","High social desirability in the profile could bias observed behavior toward more cooperative-looking moves, which may make distinguishing between genuinely prosocial and strategically prosocial behavior uncertain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"frog-orange-8868","probability":0.6},{"player":"kangaroo-red-5113","probability":0.3},{"player":"unicorn-yellow-3690","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness, empathy, and social-desirability in my profile suggest I would act cooperatively and often make or accept trades that help others; frog-orange-8868 frequently proposed trades that delivered clear gains to trade partners (e.g., PURPLE→BLUE proposals) and accepted mutually beneficial deals, matching a prosocial stance.","My high conscientiousness and tendency to engage actively would lead me to participate often and seek improving trades; kangaroo-red-5113 is the most active proposer and accepter, consistently executing profitable exchanges (steady, strategic behavior) which aligns with conscientious, engaged play.","Lower openness and moderate risk aversion imply I would avoid risky or erratic moves and sometimes decline unfavorable offers; unicorn-yellow-3690 had the fewest successful trades and declined several proposals, which fits less-engaged/cautious behavior and so is the least likely match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only observed trading actions (offers/accepts) are available; inner motives (kindness vs. calculated reciprocity) are inferred but not certain.","Players’ private chip values drive many decisions; similar actions can arise from different motives (selfish optimization vs. prosociality), making exact matching uncertain.","Social-desirability in my profile suggests stated preferences might differ from actual in-game incentives, adding ambiguity to the mapping."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"peacock-red-7894","probability":0.7},{"player":"squid-pink-8454","probability":0.25},{"player":"otter-orange-7286","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently proposed mutually beneficial trades and accepted fair offers — aligns with high agreeableness, empathy, and a fairness-oriented bargaining style (ultimatum/dictator generosity).","Moderate, steady gains rather than aggressive exploitation — matches conscientious, cooperative, caretaking tendencies and high social-desirability scores.","Accepted some others' proposals and avoided take-it-all offers, unlike the clearly exploitative proposer — fits the persona’s preference for fair outcomes and helping behavior."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only in-game actions observed; private chip valuations drive many decisions so behavior may reflect incentives rather than personality.","High social-desirability score suggests stated preferences may skew toward fairness, but actual choices could be more strategic in other contexts.","Limited rounds and stochastic tie-breaking (random partner selection) reduce the ability to perfectly infer motives from outcomes."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"otter-yellow-9944","probability":0.62},{"player":"bear-blue-8468","probability":0.28},{"player":"dog-red-9959","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness/communion and prosocial orientation: accepts mutually beneficial trades and participates cooperatively (otter accepted multiple good trades and made sensible offers).","Conscientious, fairness-minded play: otter’s proposals tended to aim for clear gains without making obviously self-harming offers; this matches the profile’s high conscientiousness and mental-accounting tendencies.","Occasional facilitating/prosocial proposer: bear made an early proposer move that generated a larger gain for a partner (consistent with a helper orientation), but also made some unprofitable proposals later (reducing match strength).","Dog’s pattern includes more self-interested or error-prone proposals that resulted in losses, which is less consistent with the profile’s careful, socially-desirable, and prosocial tendencies."],"uncertainty_notes":["We only observe a few rounds of a strategic game; proposals may reflect momentary strategy or mistakes rather than stable personality.","Private chip valuations drive many choices; similar actions can arise from different motives (pure self-interest, fairness signaling, or mistakes).","The profile’s high social-desirability score could lead to behavior that masks true preferences, making inference from limited public actions noisier."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"badger-purple-8825","probability":0.60},{"player":"otter-orange-6095","probability":0.25},{"player":"camel-yellow-4406","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness / prosociality: badger’s behavior includes accepting and proposing trades that produce small mutual gains and, in one case, accepting a slightly unfavorable trade—consistent with a cooperative, other-regarding style.","Conscientiousness and consistency: the persona is likely to make modest, fair proposals and accept trades that look reasonably balanced; badger’s accepted GREEN3-for-BLUE2 trade produced mutual benefit (small gains for both), matching that cautious, steady approach.","Generosity and trust signals: the persona’s past economic measures (high ultimatum and trust-sender offers, high dictator giving) point to someone who leans toward fair splits and cooperative proposals — badger’s proposals and acceptances align better with that than the more self-serving pattern observed for otter.","Lower willingness to reject social opportunities: camel made several proposals that were repeatedly declined and tended to only accept clearly advantageous deals, which fits less well with the persona’s high agreeableness and social-desirability orientation."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only public proposals and accept/decline outcomes are observed; private valuations (which drive decisions) differ across players and limit inference about motives.","Tie-breaking randomness and strategic complexity (e.g., accepting slightly unfavorable trades for non-monetary reasons) make behavioral attributions uncertain.","Player behavior could reflect momentary strategy or misunderstanding rather than stable personality; probabilities represent a best guess given limited, noisy evidence."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"squid-orange-6967","probability":0.7},{"player":"camel-blue-7414","probability":0.2},{"player":"lion-green-7491","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["squid frequently accepted others' proposals (including neutral/generous trades) and accepted trades that produced clear gains for partners — consistent with high agreeableness, communion, empathy, and a tendency to cooperate observed in the persona","squid's proposals produced modest mutual gains rather than aggressive one-sided gains, matching the persona's combination of prosocial preferences and pragmatic decision-making (high conscientiousness, mental-accounting orientation)","camel made repeated proposer moves that improved the proposer's payoff and declined all offers as a responder, which fits a strategic, careful player but conflicts with the persona's high agreeableness and acceptance tendencies — so camel is a weaker match","lion made bold, high-gain proposals (e.g., asking red1 for purple10) and pursued large personal gains, consistent with risk-taking/overconfident behavior — this contrasts with the persona's stronger communal orientation, so lion is the least likely match"],"uncertainty_notes":["Only a few rounds of behavior are observed; small sample may not reflect stable personality traits","Players had different private chip values, so some accept/decline choices may be driven primarily by private payoffs rather than underlying social or personality preferences","High social-desirability and other self-report measures in the persona can drive behavior that looks cooperative but might be strategic in the game context; mapping observed moves to internal traits has ambiguity"]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"whale-pink-487","probability":0.60},{"player":"duck-red-8937","probability":0.30},{"player":"iguana-red-1430","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Repeatedly proposed trades that produced clear gains for partners as well as herself (generous/cooperative proposer behavior).","Consistent, successful trading strategy (conscientious, goal-directed).","High acceptance of mutually beneficial offers by other players suggests a cooperative social style—matches high agreeableness/empathy in profile."],"uncertainty_notes":["Chip valuations are private; interpretation relies on observed offers and acceptances, which can reflect strategic exploitation of value differences rather than pure prosociality.","All three players showed some cooperative and some self-interested moves; overlap makes precise matching uncertain.","Context (e.g., motives, risk preferences) can’t be observed directly; probabilities reflect plausibility, not certainty."]}
